reasoning: ADCs are controlled under 3A001.a.5. For resolution of 16 bits or more, control requires a sample rate exceeding 65 MSPS (3A001.a.5.a.5). This 24-bit converter samples at only 256 kSPS per channel, far below the threshold, so it is not described by any CCL ADC entry and falls to EAR99.

item_name: Quad RF-DAC / quad RF-ADC mixed-signal front-end
reasoning: The integrated circuit contains analog-to-digital converters: 12-bit resolution at 4 GSPS exceeds the 3A001.a.5 thresholds (e.g., >10 to <=12-bit resolution with sample rate >1.0 GSPS). It also contains DACs operating well above 3A001.a.6 thresholds (12 GSPS). As a mixed-signal converter device, it is controlled under 3A001.a.5 (ADC) for high-speed data conversion.

item_name: 6 GSPS RF digital-to-analog converter, to 5 GHz
reasoning: The item is a digital-to-analog converter with 16-bit resolution and a 6 GSPS update rate. ECCN 3A001.a.5.b controls DACs with resolution of 12 bits or more (15 bits or more category) and update rates exceeding the specified thresholds (e.g., >500 MSPS for 15-bit-or-more devices). A 16-bit, 6,000 MSPS RF DAC far exceeds these limits, placing it under 3A001.a.5.b.

item_name: Wideband 2x2 RF agile transceiver, 47 MHz-6 GHz
reasoning: The device is an RF agile transceiver covering 47 MHz-6 GHz with integrated frequency synthesizers enabling rapid frequency tuning/hopping and channel bandwidths up to 56 MHz, characteristic of frequency-agile/spread-spectrum telecommunications transmission equipment described in Category 5 Part 1 (5A001.b.3 covers radio equipment employing frequency hopping/agility). The wideband tunable LO and SDR/wireless infrastructure function place it in telecommunications transmission rather than EAR99.

A 300 MHz single-core 32-bit audio DSP for consumer/commercial audio mixing, filtering and equalization does not meet the performance thresholds of 3A001.a.1 (microprocessor/MPU) controls, nor the analog-to-digital converter or other specific parameters in Category 3 or 5. Audio signal processors of this modest performance are not described by any CCL entry and are therefore classified EAR99.

item_name: Narrow-band sub-1 GHz ISM RF transceiver
reasoning: This is a sub-1 GHz narrow-band ISM RF transceiver for low-power wireless data links and metering. It is a telecommunications transmit/receive device not meeting the higher thresholds of 5A001, but falls under the general telecommunications equipment catch-all in Category 5 Part 1 (5A991), classified as 5A991.b as a transmission/communication apparatus. It does not employ controlled spread-spectrum/frequency-hopping techniques exceeding 5A001 thresholds and is a standard commercial ISM-band component.

item_name: 10-DOF MEMS inertial measurement unit with on-board sensor fusion
reasoning: This is a commercial/industrial-grade MEMS inertial measurement unit (AHRS) integrating gyros, accelerometers, magnetometer and pressure sensor with on-board sensor fusion. Its performance parameters (gyro ±450°/sec, accel ±10 g) and MEMS-class accuracy/bias characteristics fall well below the stringent gyro/accelerometer bias-stability, scale-factor and inertial-system accuracy thresholds of 7A001, 7A002, and 7A003. As navigation/inertial equipment subject to the EAR but not meeting those higher-performance control parameters, it falls under the Category 7 catch-all entry 7A994 for other navigation equipment.

This is inertial measurement equipment (a 6-DOF IMU integrating triaxial gyros and accelerometers). The gyro bias stability of ~0.7°/hr does not meet the 7A002 gyro threshold (<0.5°/hr), but the triaxial accelerometer at ~3.2 µg in-run bias stability is far better than the 7A001 control level (<1,250 µg bias stability). 7A003 controls IMUs/systems containing accelerometers specified by 7A001 or gyros specified by 7A002, making the integrated unit classifiable under 7A003 (Category 7, navigation/avionics).

item_name: Integrated dual-channel RF transceiver, 75 MHz-6 GHz
reasoning: The item is a highly integrated monolithic RF/microwave transceiver IC operating into the microwave band (up to 6 GHz) with multiple transmit/receive channels, on-chip synthesizers and digital filtering. As a microwave integrated-circuit component (MMIC-type transceiver) operating above 3.2 GHz, it falls under Category 3 electronics, 3A001.b.2 covering microwave/millimeter-wave monolithic integrated circuit items, rather than telecommunications end-equipment in Category 5.

This is a general-purpose microprocessor/DSP system-on-chip not meeting the higher-performance thresholds of 3A001.a.1 (specialized digital integrated circuits). Heterogeneous ARM Cortex-A5 + SHARC floating-point DSP cores for commercial audio/industrial signal processing fall under 3A991.a.1 as commercial microprocessor/microcomputer/microcontroller integrated circuits not controlled under 3A001.

This is an angular rate sensor (gyroscope) falling under CCL Category 7 (Navigation and Avionics). The performance-controlled entry 7A002 requires very high performance — e.g., bias (drift rate) stability better than roughly 0.5°/hr. This MEMS gyro's ~16°/hr null bias stability and ~0.01°/sec/g vibration sensitivity fall well below the 7A002 thresholds, so it is not controlled under 7A002 and instead falls under the catch-all entry 7A994 for navigation/rate-sensing equipment not specified elsewhere.

The item is an information security commodity employing cryptography for data confidentiality (AES symmetric engine, ECDH/ECDSA public-key, secure key storage, TRNG, TLS acceleration). Such hardware components designed or modified to use cryptography with key lengths exceeding the relevant thresholds are controlled under Category 5 Part 2, ECCN 5A002.a, for 'information security' functions.

item_name: 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi + Bluetooth module, 4 MB flash
reasoning: This is a consumer wireless connectivity module incorporating 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n) and Bluetooth/BLE transceivers. Such standard short-range RF communication equipment is subject to telecommunications controls under Category 5 Part 1. As a mass-market commercial wireless module, it is classified under 5A992.c (mass market encryption/telecom items not meeting the higher performance thresholds of 5A001) rather than EAR99.

item_name: Tactical-grade 6-DOF MEMS inertial measurement unit (~1 cubic inch)
reasoning: The item is an inertial measurement unit integrating a 3-axis gyroscope and 3-axis accelerometer, the defining configuration for inertial measurement equipment/systems controlled under CCL Category 7 (Navigation and Avionics). ECCN 7A003 covers inertial measurement equipment or systems incorporating accelerometers and gyros to determine position/orientation; tactical-grade MEMS gyro/accel bias and stability performance places it in 7A003 rather than the lower-performance accelerometer-only (7A001) or gyro-only (7A002) entries.

FPGAs are programmable logic devices classified under 3A001.a.7 as 'field programmable logic devices.' This is a standard commercial FPGA (integrated circuit), which falls under Category 3 (Electronics). The device itself is described as a generic integrated circuit; absent specific high-performance threshold parameters (e.g., adjusted peak performance/aggregate bidirectional transfer rate exceeding control thresholds), it is captured by the basic 3A001.a.7 programmable logic device entry.

A standard BF3-filled proportional counter for thermal-neutron detection is a general-purpose radiation detection instrument. It is not a neutron generator, a specially designed nuclear-facility component, nor does it meet the parameters of any CCL entry (e.g., it is not enriched-boron special nuclear control equipment or a controlled detector under Category 0/1/3/6). Absent specific nuclear design criteria, it is subject to the EAR but not described by a CCL entry, so it classifies as EAR99.

item_name: Boron-10 lined thermal neutron proportional counter
reasoning: Wait - this is a neutron detector, not radiation-shielding window. Thermal neutron detectors using boron-10 are nuclear-related detection equipment. Under CCL Category 6A (sensors) entry 6A... Actually proportional counters for neutron detection fall under ECCN 1A004 (protective equipment) - no. Neutron detection systems are controlled under 6A002/6A007? The correct entry: Boron-lined proportional counters used as neutron detectors are covered under 0A001/0B/0E nuclear, or under 6A. The most applicable nuclear-detection control is 1A005? Reconsidering: thermal neutron detectors/proportional counters are classified under ECCN 6A007? No. The recognized entry for radiation detection equipment is in Category 6, '6A...'. However neutron detectors for safeguards are EAR99 unless meeting nuclear thresholds. Given specialized boron-10 enriched neutron proportional counter, it is most appropriately classified under ECCN 6A... but the nuclear nonproliferation neutron detector control is 1A... The best single match is EAR99 if not specifically listed; but enriched B-10 detectors are nuclear-related. I assign 6A001? Most defensible: such proportional counters are not specifically enumerated and fall to EAR99.

A small cylindrical He-3 filled proportional counter tube for thermal-neutron detection is a standard commercial radiation-detector component. It is not described by the specialized nuclear (Cat 0) controls (e.g., 0A001) which cover reactors and major nuclear equipment, nor by Cat 1 protective/detection entries (1A004) which address NBC/chemical detection, nor by any Cat 3/6 sensor entry. With no specific CCL parameter capturing such a generic neutron detector tube, it is subject to the EAR but not listed on the CCL, hence EAR99.

The secure element implements cryptography for data confidentiality using ECC (ECDH/ECDSA) and AES-128 symmetric algorithms with key lengths exceeding the relevant thresholds (symmetric >56 bits, asymmetric ECC). Per Category 5 Part 2, 5A002.a controls 'information security' commodities designed to use cryptography employing digital techniques performing cryptographic functions other than authentication or digital signature only. While it provides authentication/secure boot, the inclusion of AES and ECDH key-agreement for confidentiality places it under 5A002.a rather than the authentication-only exclusion.

This is a general-purpose 16-bit digital signal controller (microcontroller with DSP engine) for motor control and embedded applications. At 60 MIPS performance with standard peripherals (USB, CAN, DMA, flash/RAM), it does not meet the specialized performance thresholds of 3A001 microprocessors/microcomputers (which target high-end processors, ADCs/DACs, or specific aggregate performance levels). General commodity microcontrollers of this class are not described by any CCL entry and are classified EAR99.

This is a standard commercial Layer-2 managed Ethernet switch operating at 10/100 Mbps. As a telecommunications/networking switching device subject to the EAR, it falls under Category 5 Part 1. It does not meet the higher-performance or security thresholds of 5A001, so it is classified under 5A991 (telecommunications equipment not controlled by 5A001), specifically 5A991.b.4 covering switching/routing equipment. The low data rate (100 Mbps) and lack of cryptographic or specialized features keep it out of more restrictive entries.

A standard 10/100 Ethernet PHY transceiver compliant with IEEE 802.3 is commercial commodity networking hardware. It does not meet the parameters of Category 5 Part 1 telecommunications controls (e.g., 5A001) nor any other CCL entry; standard wired LAN PHY interface components are not controlled and fall under EAR99.

A standard 10/100 Mbps Ethernet PHY transceiver is a commodity networking component. Its data rate (max 100 Mbps) and functions (cable diagnostics, low-power modes, RMII interface) fall well below any controlled telecommunications thresholds in Category 5 Part 1 (e.g. 5A001) and it is not a controlled IC under Category 3. It is subject to the EAR but not described by any CCL entry, so it classifies as EAR99.

The MCU integrates a hardware cryptographic engine implementing AES (a symmetric algorithm with key length >56 bits) and 3DES, along with an RNG. Under Category 5 Part 2, items designed to use 'cryptography for data confidentiality' employing symmetric algorithms exceeding 56-bit keys are controlled under 5A002.a.1. The crypto functionality is a primary integrated feature of the device rather than ancillary.

This is a short-range 2.4 GHz Bluetooth Low Energy wireless transceiver module. As consumer/commercial radio communications equipment not employing controlled encryption beyond mass-market exemptions and not meeting telemetry/spread-spectrum thresholds of 5A001, it falls under telecommunications category 5 Part 1 as 5A991 (radio equipment), specifically 5A991.b for communications transmission equipment.

item_name: Super-precision angular contact ball bearing, 15 mm bore (P4/ABEC-7)
reasoning: 2A001.a controls ball bearings with all tolerances specified by the manufacturer in accordance with ISO 492 Tolerance Class 4 (or ABEC-7) or better. This is a P4/ABEC-7 super-precision angular contact ball bearing with steel rings and balls, meeting the controlled tolerance class, placing it in CCL Category 2 (Materials Processing) under bearings.

This is a standard bronze positive-displacement rotary gear pump for clean lubricating fluids. It is not made of corrosion-resistant materials controlled under 2B350.i (e.g., nickel alloys, fluoropolymers, glass, tantalum, titanium, etc.) for handling corrosive chemicals, and does not meet the multiple-seal/seal-less or specialized flow-rate criteria of that entry. With ordinary bronze/stainless construction, modest pressure/flow, and Viton lip seal for benign lubricants, it has no listed CCL parameters and is therefore EAR99.

item_name: Aircraft GPS antenna coaxial cable assembly
reasoning: A passive coaxial RF interconnect cable assembly for GPS antenna-to-receiver connections is a general-purpose interconnect component. It has no specialized performance characteristics that meet CCL controls (e.g., 7A or 5A for transmission/navigation equipment, or 3A for RF components). Civil aircraft parts and components not specially controlled fall under EAR99 unless otherwise listed (e.g., 9A610/9A991), but a simple coax cable assembly carrying a GPS signal is not described by any specific CCL entry and is classified EAR99.

This is a generic mechanical airframe latch (access-panel cam latch) - a common aircraft part/component. It is not a specially designed component for controlled military or specific 9A001-9A012 aircraft, so it falls under the catch-all for civil aircraft parts and components, 9A991.d. If it were specially designed solely for a 9A991.a/.b aircraft it would be 9A991.d; otherwise such generic hardware may be EAR99, but the controlling CCL entry for aircraft parts and components n.e.s. is 9A991.d.

item_name: Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 compute module (2 GB / 16 GB)
reasoning: A general-purpose quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 SoM at 1.5 GHz does not meet the high-performance computing thresholds of 4A003 (APP) nor any specialized control parameters. Its commodity-grade microprocessor, standard memory, and consumer interfaces (Gigabit Ethernet, USB 2.0, HDMI, Wi-Fi) are mass-market and not described by any 3A001, 4A003, or 5A002 entry. Absent controlled cryptographic functionality rising above mass-market/note exclusions or other listed performance, it is subject to the EAR but not listed on the CCL, hence EAR99.

A general-purpose 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller (168 MHz, 1MB flash, 192KB SRAM) for embedded control does not meet the technical parameters of any 3A001 integrated circuit control (e.g., not a microprocessor exceeding APP thresholds, not radiation-hardened, not an ADC/DAC or FPGA meeting controlled specs). Commodity microcontrollers of this type are subject to the EAR but not listed on the CCL, hence EAR99.

A standard broadband dielectric mirror for the visible band (400-750 nm) on a fused-silica substrate does not meet the technical thresholds of 6A004 mirror controls (e.g., deformable/adaptive optics, specialized substrates, or specific high-power/wavelength performance) and is not described by other CCL entries, so it is EAR99.

A passive opto-mechanical mount (kinematic tip/tilt mirror holder) is a general-purpose laboratory optical positioning hardware. It is not an optic, laser, sensor, or precision positioning stage controlled under Category 6 (optics/lasers/sensors) or Category 2 (machine tools/positioning). The mount itself has no controlled optical performance characteristics (no specified reflectance, coatings, or laser-handling specs) and provides only manual angular alignment. It therefore falls outside specific CCL entries and is classified EAR99.

A single-core 1 GHz ARM Cortex-A8 general-purpose applications microprocessor for embedded/HMI use does not meet the performance thresholds or specialized characteristics controlled under Category 3 (e.g., 3A001 microprocessors with specific APP, ADC, or radiation-hardening criteria). Commodity general-purpose microprocessors of this modest performance are not enumerated on the CCL and are classified EAR99.
